items.prompt: Without looking back, describe the recommended Premed subject journey to a new learner in your own words. In the opening, identify both inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work. Name at least three middle foundation areas, including at least one chemical or physical area (general chemistry, physics, or organic chemistry) and at least one biological area (biochemistry or biology/genetics). Name at least two later medicine-facing domains (human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, or behaviour/society/population health), and identify integration or capstone work as the final stage. Give at least two explicit because/how links showing how one named stage prepares another; order alone is not enough.

answer.correct: A complete response identifies inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work at the opening; correctly places and names at least three middle groups from general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, and biology/genetics, including at least one chemical or physical group and at least one biological group; names at least two later domains from human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, and behaviour/society/population health; names integration/capstone as the final stage; and explains at least two accurate preparation links.
answer.summary: The journey moves from trustworthy evidence and measurement through matter and living systems to people, populations, and an unfamiliar capstone problem.
answer.reasoning: Inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work come first because later subjects use measurements and evidence. The middle needs enough correctly placed chemical, physical, and biological breadth to show how matter and living systems are developed, without requiring every label. The later stage needs representative medicine-facing domains before integration and capstone work bring the foundations together. Two explicit because/how links show preparation rather than chronology alone. Assessment mastery requires 10/10, so every criterion in all three rubrics must be met. If any criterion is missed, use the corresponding recovery route and retry. On retry, advise an incoming learner how to proceed from trustworthy evidence to a capstone, still naming representative subject areas at each stage without reproducing the original response.
acceptableResponses: Prose, bullets, or an outline using ordinary-language equivalents that meets the stated opening, representative middle, representative later, final-stage, and two-link requirements while preserving the broad recommended chronology.
commonErrors: Giving an unordered subject list with no explanation of why the order helps
Naming too few representative middle or later subject groups, or omitting integration and capstone work as the final stage
Beginning with advanced medical content instead of inquiry, laboratory, and quantitative work

items.prompt: Choose four representative concepts from four different Premed subject areas. In plain language, explain what each concept helps unlock for later medical study; all four concept-to-purpose links are required. You are describing relevance, not teaching the detailed science.

answer.correct: The response identifies four representative concepts from four different subject areas and gives a defensible later-study link for each, such as uncertainty to research interpretation, pressure and flow to circulation, molecular shape to biomolecule behaviour, gene expression to cell differences, or social conditions to population health patterns.
answer.summary: Representative concepts earn their place by enabling later explanations, measurements, or evidence judgments.
answer.reasoning: This task asks for a concept-to-purpose relationship. Naming a subject is not enough; each example must say what the concept later helps explain or evaluate. Assessment mastery requires 10/10, so every criterion in all three rubrics must be met. If any criterion is missed, use the corresponding recovery route and retry. A retry should use different subjects or concepts so that the learner reconstructs the relationship instead of copying feedback.
acceptableResponses: Any four accurate concept-and-relevance pairs drawn from four different named subject areas in the lesson.
commonErrors: Listing concepts without saying what they unlock
Making clinical claims instead of describing preparation for study
Using four concepts from only one subject

items.prompt: A learner says that completing Premed means remembering a large set of facts. Correct that account. State the four main completion capabilities, give one concrete action that shows each capability, and state one thing course completion does not authorize, award, or guarantee.

answer.correct: Completion requires explaining scientific relationships, modelling them with appropriate representations, investigating questions with auditable methods, and integrating several subjects in unfamiliar problems. Each capability must be tied to an observable action, and the response must state one accurate boundary: completion does not authorize diagnosis or treatment, award university credit or a professional licence, guarantee admission, confer clinical competence, or grant professional authority.
answer.summary: Premed aims at explanation, modelling, investigation, and integration rather than recall alone.
answer.reasoning: Facts support the four capabilities but do not replace them. Concrete evidence includes explaining a relationship, using and limiting a graph or model, designing and documenting a fair investigation, and connecting several domains in a new problem. The explicit completion boundary prevents a study-preparation course from being mistaken for a credential or professional authorization. Assessment mastery requires 10/10, so every criterion in all three rubrics must be met. If any criterion is missed, use the corresponding recovery route and retry. On retry, apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario, give one action for each, and include one accurate completion boundary rather than repeating the original response.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording that identifies explain, model, investigate, and integrate, gives an observable example of each, and states one accurate limit on what completion authorizes, awards, or guarantees.
commonErrors: Treating course completion as recall or time spent
Naming the four verbs without concrete actions
Claiming that completion grants clinical competence, credit, admission, a professional licence, or professional authority

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when the response identifies both inquiry or laboratory work and quantitative work in the opening stage, accepting ordinary-language equivalents; award 0 if either kind of work is missing or the opening is described only as unspecified basics.

Award 1 point only when the response correctly places and names at least three of these five middle subject groups—general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, and biology/genetics. For this rule, at least one named group must be general chemistry, physics, or organic chemistry, and at least one must be biochemistry or biology/genetics. Award 0 if fewer than three groups are named, either required breadth category is absent, or the groups are placed outside the middle stage.

Award 1 point only when the response names at least two of these three later domains—human structure/physiology, microbiology/immunology, and behaviour/society/population health—and also names integration/capstone as the final stage; award 0 if fewer than two later domains are named, they are not placed in the later stage, or integration/capstone is not named as final.

Award 1 point only when at least two named stage pairs are joined by explicit, accurate because/how relationships explaining preparation; award 0 for fewer than two such links, or for restating order without explaining preparation.

Award 1 point only when four distinct concepts are each paired with a different named Premed subject area; award 0 if there are fewer than four concepts, fewer than four subject areas, or subject names without concepts.

Award 1 point only when every one of the four concepts has an accurate, specific explanation of how it supports later medical study; award 0 if any concept lacks a purpose or is linked only by a vague claim that it matters.

Award 1 point only when all four links are understandable in plain language, remain at orientation depth, and make no unsupported clinical claim; award 0 if jargon hides a link, detailed untaught science is asserted, or any clinical claim exceeds preparation for study.

Award 1 point only when the response identifies all four capabilities—explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating, or clear equivalents; award 0 if any capability is missing or replaced by recall alone.

Award 1 point only when each of the four capabilities has at least one observable scientific action that demonstrates it; award 0 if any capability has no action or is illustrated only by reading, remembering, or spending time.

Award 1 point only when the response explicitly states one accurate thing course completion does not authorize, award, or guarantee, consistent with the lesson's credit, admission, clinical-competence, diagnosis/treatment, professional-licence, or professional-authority boundaries. Award 0 when this requested boundary is absent because the evidence is missing. Also award 0 when the response contradicts the boundary by claiming any such entitlement; flag that contradiction for correction rather than treating it as a mere omission.
